---

## 1) Current lineup most relevant to model selection

OpenAI’s current docs surface the following relevant model families:

### Flagship GPT family
- `gpt-5.5`
- `gpt-5.5-pro`
- `gpt-5.4`
- `gpt-5.4-pro`
- `gpt-5.4-mini`
- `gpt-5.4-nano`

OpenAI’s pricing page groups these under **Flagship models**.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

### Earlier GPT models still documented
- `gpt-5`
- `gpt-5-mini`
- `gpt-5.1`

These still have model pages, but OpenAI’s current docs recommend newer models for many use cases. For example:
- GPT-5 page: “We recommend using the latest GPT-5.5.”  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5
- GPT-5 mini page: “For most new low-latency, high-volume workloads, we recommend starting with GPT-5.4 mini.”  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5-mini

### Reasoning / specialized models still relevant for comparison
- `o3`
- `o3-deep-research`
- `o4-mini-deep-research`

The reasoning guide also explicitly distinguishes **reasoning models** (for example `o3` and `o4-mini`) from **GPT models**.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning-best-practices

---

## 2) Current API pricing table

Below is the pricing that is clearly surfaced on OpenAI’s current pricing page for the lineup most relevant to engineering planning.

## Flagship and practical mid-tier choices

| Model | Exact API model name | Input / 1M tokens | Cached input / 1M | Output / 1M | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| GPT-5.5 | `gpt-5.5` | $5.00 | $0.50 | $30.00 | Premium frontier model |
| GPT-5.5 Pro | `gpt-5.5-pro` | $30.00 | not listed | $180.00 | Highest-cost premium tier |
| GPT-5.4 | `gpt-5.4` | $2.50 | $0.25 | $15.00 | Lower-cost flagship |
| GPT-5.4 Pro | `gpt-5.4-pro` | $30.00 | not listed | $180.00 | Premium “thinks harder” option |
| GPT-5.4 mini | `gpt-5.4-mini` | $0.75 | $0.075 | $4.50 | Best practical mid-tier |
| GPT-5.4 nano | `gpt-5.4-nano` | $0.20 | $0.02 | $1.25 | Cheapest current GPT-family option |

Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

## Specialized research models

| Model | Exact API model name | Input / 1M tokens | Cached input / 1M | Output / 1M | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| o3 Deep Research | `o3-deep-research` | $5.00 | not listed | $20.00 | Specialized research workflow model |
| o4-mini Deep Research | `o4-mini-deep-research` | $1.00 | not listed | $4.00 | Lower-cost research workflow model |

Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

### Important pricing caveat
For the Pro and Deep Research rows above, the pricing page snippet surfaced by official search did **not** expose cached-input pricing. This brief does **not** infer a cached-input price when OpenAI’s pricing page does not clearly list one.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

---

## 3) Reasoning-effort options

OpenAI’s reasoning guide confirms that reasoning-capable models support a `reasoning.effort` control in the Responses API. The guide uses `gpt-5.5` in examples:

```python
response = client.responses.create(
  model="gpt-5.5",
  reasoning={"effort": "low"},
  input=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
)
```

Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning

### What is explicitly confirmed in current docs

- OpenAI supports a `reasoning.effort` parameter for reasoning-capable models.  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning

- OpenAI introduced a `minimal` reasoning-effort value for GPT-5 models that support reasoning, to optimize for faster responses.  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/changelog

- GPT-5 is described as a “previous intelligent reasoning model” with **configurable reasoning effort**.  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5

- GPT-5.1 is documented as supporting **configurable reasoning and non-reasoning effort**.  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.1

### What can be stated safely for planning

For **reasoning-capable GPT-5 models**, the currently verified reasoning-effort options are:

- `minimal` — explicitly added in the changelog for GPT-5 models that support reasoning  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/changelog
- `low` — explicitly shown in the reasoning guide example with `gpt-5.5`  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning

OpenAI’s currently retrieved official snippets did **not** cleanly list the full option set per current model page, so this brief avoids overstating support for `medium` or `high` across every current model unless directly documented on the page in question.

### Practical implication
If your team plans to use **reasoning effort as a cost/latency dial**, prioritize models whose docs explicitly support configurable reasoning:
- `gpt-5.5`
- `gpt-5`
- `gpt-5.1`

Source pages:
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.1

---

## 4) Model-by-model planning guidance

## A. `gpt-5.5` — premium default for difficult work
OpenAI describes GPT-5.5 as its newest frontier model for complex professional work. It also appears in the reasoning guide as a reasoning-capable model.  
Sources:
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.5
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning

**Use when:**
- complex coding help
- multi-step agent workflows
- quality matters more than token spend
- you want reasoning control

**Cost profile:**
- Input: $5.00 / 1M
- Cached input: $0.50 / 1M
- Output: $30.00 / 1M  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Important caveat:**  
For prompts with **more than 272K input tokens**, OpenAI states GPT-5.5 is priced at **2x input** and **1.5x output** for the full session.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.5

**Recommendation:**  
Make this the benchmark model for evals, not necessarily the default for all production traffic.

---

## B. `gpt-5.4` — strongest value flagship
OpenAI describes GPT-5.4 as a more affordable model for coding and professional work.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4

**Use when:**
- you need near-frontier quality at lower cost than GPT-5.5
- coding, tool use, and professional text tasks dominate
- you want a strong default before dropping to mini/nano

**Cost profile:**
- Input: $2.50 / 1M
- Cached input: $0.25 / 1M
- Output: $15.00 / 1M  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Long-context caveat:**  
For GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.4 Pro, OpenAI states that prompts above **272K input tokens** on the **1.05M context window** models are priced at **2x input** and **1.5x output** for the full session.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4

**Recommendation:**  
For many teams, this is the most balanced flagship for production.

---

## C. `gpt-5.4-mini` — best practical mid-tier workhorse
OpenAI describes GPT-5.4 mini as bringing GPT-5.4 strengths to a faster, more efficient model for high-volume workloads.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4-mini

**Use when:**
- high request volume
- lower latency requirements
- cost-sensitive product surfaces
- agent/tool workflows where many calls are “good enough” rather than frontier-critical

**Cost profile:**
- Input: $0.75 / 1M
- Cached input: $0.075 / 1M
- Output: $4.50 / 1M  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Recommendation:**  
This is the strongest candidate for a team’s **default production model** if cost discipline matters.

---

## D. `gpt-5.4-nano` — budget tier
OpenAI lists GPT-5.4 nano in flagship pricing at the lowest visible current GPT-family price point.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Use when:**
- large-scale classification/routing
- structured extraction
- lightweight transforms
- high-volume internal automations

**Cost profile:**
- Input: $0.20 / 1M
- Cached input: $0.02 / 1M
- Output: $1.25 / 1M  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Recommendation:**  
Use this for “cheap first pass” stages, not for highest-stakes final outputs unless evals support it.

---

## E. `gpt-5.5-pro` and `gpt-5.4-pro` — reserve for narrow high-value paths
OpenAI describes the Pro variants as versions that produce smarter and more precise responses.  
Sources:
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.5-pro
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4-pro

**Cost profile:**
- `gpt-5.5-pro`: $30.00 input / 1M, $180.00 output / 1M
- `gpt-5.4-pro`: $30.00 input / 1M, $180.00 output / 1M  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Use when:**
- expert review
- hardest coding/planning tasks
- small-volume human-in-the-loop workflows
- expensive mistakes justify much higher model cost

**Recommendation:**  
Do not use these as general defaults. Gate them behind routing or escalation.

---

## F. `o3` and deep-research variants
OpenAI’s docs still surface `o3` as a reasoning model, but note that it was succeeded by GPT-5.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/o3

The pricing page currently surfaces:
- `o3-deep-research`
- `o4-mini-deep-research`  
as specialized models.  
Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Recommendation:**  
Treat these as specialized workflow models, not default replacements for GPT-5.x text generation.

---

## 5) Cost selection patterns for engineering teams

## Recommended default stack

### Tier 1: benchmark / hardest tasks
- `gpt-5.5`

Use for:
- hardest coding tasks
- complex bug analysis
- planning across large codebases
- top-quality agent decisions

### Tier 2: main production default
- `gpt-5.4` or `gpt-5.4-mini`

Use:
- `gpt-5.4` when quality is more important than cost
- `gpt-5.4-mini` when cost and throughput matter more

### Tier 3: cheap first-pass / background work
- `gpt-5.4-nano`

Use for:
- intent classification
- triage
- extraction
- summarization before escalation
- prefiltering before a larger model

---

## 6) Budget implications

Two cost drivers stand out in OpenAI’s current pricing:

### A. Output tokens are much more expensive than input
Example:
- `gpt-5.5`: output is **6x** input price ($30 vs $5)
- `gpt-5.4`: output is **6x** input price ($15 vs $2.5)
- `gpt-5.4-mini`: output is **6x** input price ($4.5 vs $0.75)

Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Implication:**  
The biggest savings often come from:
- limiting verbosity
- reducing unnecessary chain length
- constraining format
- using structured outputs when possible

### B. Prompt caching matters a lot
OpenAI publishes sharply reduced cached-input rates for several flagship models:
- `gpt-5.5`: $0.50 vs $5.00 input
- `gpt-5.4`: $0.25 vs $2.50 input
- `gpt-5.4-mini`: $0.075 vs $0.75 input
- `gpt-5.4-nano`: $0.02 vs $0.20 input

Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

**Implication:**  
If your app reuses system prompts, policy prompts, tool schemas, or large repeated context blocks, prompt caching can materially reduce spend.

### C. Very long contexts can trigger pricing uplifts
- GPT-5.5: >272K input tokens incurs 2x input and 1.5x output for the session  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.5
- GPT-5.4 / GPT-5.4 Pro: same threshold behavior on 1.05M context models  
  Source: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/gpt-5.4

**Implication:**  
Do not assume “large context” is free. Retrieval and compaction may be cheaper than repeatedly sending huge sessions.

---

## 7) Recommended selection policy

A practical routing policy for 2026 engineering workloads:

1. **Default to `gpt-5.4-mini`** for most product flows.
2. **Escalate to `gpt-5.4`** when evals show mini underperforms.
3. **Escalate to `gpt-5.5`** only for tasks where quality gains justify ~2x the GPT-5.4 input/output price and much more vs mini.
4. **Use `gpt-5.4-nano`** for cheap first-pass steps.
5. **Restrict Pro models** to small-volume, high-value workflows.
6. **Use reasoning effort selectively** on reasoning-capable GPT-5 models, because more thinking usually trades off against latency and possibly downstream cost exposure.  
   Reasoning docs: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning

---

## 8) Final recommendation

If your team wants a simple starting point:

- **Flagship default for highest quality:** `gpt-5.5`
- **Best value flagship:** `gpt-5.4`
- **Best mid-tier/high-volume default:** `gpt-5.4-mini`
- **Best budget utility model:** `gpt-5.4-nano`
- **Use Pro only by exception**
- **Use reasoning-effort controls on reasoning-capable GPT-5 models when the extra thinking is worth the latency/cost tradeoff**

All pricing and lineup details above are grounded in OpenAI’s current developer docs:
- Pricing: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing
- Models index: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/all
- Reasoning guide: https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning

Short answer: as of mid-2026, the key current OpenAI API choices are `gpt-5.5`, `gpt-5.4`, `gpt-5.4-mini`, and `gpt-5.4-nano`; `gpt-5.4-mini` is the best practical mid-tier default, while `gpt-5.5` is the premium flagship and the pricing source of truth is OpenAI’s official pricing page.

records.prompt: Write a migration guide for our engineering team: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to the current Next.js as of mid-2026. Cover the current version status, runtime requirements, breaking changes, and new build tooling defaults. Be specific about versions and API names.

baseline.report:
# Migration guide: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to current Next.js in mid-2026

## Executive summary

As of mid-2026, the current stable documentation version is **Next.js 16.2.9**, while **Next.js 16.3** is still a **Preview** release as of late June 2026, not the stable baseline you should target for a production migration plan yet. Official blog and docs sources show:

- **Next.js 16** released on **2025-10-21**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16
- **Next.js 16.1** released on **2025-12-18**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1
- **Next.js 16.2** released on **2026-03-18**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2
- The docs “Latest Version” shown in the official upgrade docs is **16.2.9**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- **Next.js 16.3** was announced as a **Preview** on **2026-06-25/29**, so it should be treated as pre-stable for most production teams.  
  Sources:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-turbopack

**Recommendation:** upgrade production apps from Next.js 15.x to **Next.js 16.2.x** first, not 16.3 Preview.

---

## 1) Current version status as of mid-2026

### Recommended target
Target **Next.js 16.2.x**, because that is the latest stable major/minor line evidenced by official docs and release posts as of mid-2026.

- Docs show “Latest Version **16.2.9**”:  
  https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- Stable 16.2 release post:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2

### Do not treat 16.3 as stable yet
The June 2026 posts explicitly frame 16.3 as a **Preview**:

- “Next.js 16.3 Preview”:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations
- Turbopack improvements “as the Next.js 16.3 Preview nears a stable release”:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-turbopack

### Practical implication
For production apps, your upgrade path should be:

1. **Normalize on the latest 15.x first** if you are behind within 15.x.
2. **Upgrade to 16.2.x stable**.
3. Evaluate **16.3 Preview** only in a branch or non-production environment.

---

## 2) Runtime and platform requirements

Next.js 16 raises baseline requirements.

### Node.js
Next.js 16 requires **Node.js 20.9.0+**; **Node.js 18 is no longer supported**.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements

### TypeScript
Next.js 16 requires **TypeScript 5.1.0+**.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements

### Browsers
Supported browser baseline in Next.js 16:

- Chrome **111+**
- Edge **111+**
- Firefox **111+**
- Safari **16.4+**

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements

### React
Next.js 15 already moved the minimum React baseline to **React 19**.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

Next.js 16’s App Router uses the latest React canary line incorporating React 19.2 features in that release train.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#react-192-and-canary-features

### Operational advice
Before changing Next.js itself, update your deployment runtime and CI images to:

- Node **20.9+**
- TypeScript **5.1+**
- current React / React DOM compatible with Next 16
- current `@types/react` and `@types/react-dom` if you use TS

Relevant sources:  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#version-requirements  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

---

## 3) The safest upgrade strategy for a production team

## Phase 0: align to the latest 15.x first
If your app is not already on a recent 15.x patch/minor, first update within 15.x. This reduces the number of moving parts and surfaces deprecations earlier.

Why: Next.js 15.5 explicitly added deprecation warnings for Next.js 16-era changes and made Turbopack builds more mature.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

## Phase 1: run the official upgrade tooling
Use the official upgrade flow.

### If already on 16.1.0+
You can use:

```bash
pnpm next upgrade
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading

### From 15.x to 16.x
Use the codemod-based upgrade path:

```bash
npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
```

Or explicitly target 16:

```bash
npx @next/codemod upgrade 16
```

- Sources:  
  https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading  
  https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Important codemods for this migration
Relevant official codemods include:

- `remove-experimental-ppr`
- `remove-unstable-prefix`
- `middleware-to-proxy`
- `next-lint-to-eslint-cli`

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

## Phase 2: make runtime/platform changes first
Upgrade infra before app behavior:

- Node 20.9+
- TypeScript 5.1+
- CI images / Docker base images
- hosting adapter expectations if self-hosting

## Phase 3: migrate breaking APIs
Focus first on things that can break builds or request handling:

- `middleware.ts` / `proxy.ts`
- async request APIs
- `revalidateTag()` signature
- custom webpack config behavior under Turbopack default
- required `default.js` for parallel routes
- removed config flags / deprecated config locations

## Phase 4: validate prod behavior
Regression-test:

- auth/session
- rewrites/redirects
- image optimization
- cache invalidation
- route handlers
- build system / CI caching
- custom webpack plugins/loaders
- lint pipeline

---

## 4) Breaking changes you must account for

## 4.1 `middleware.ts` is deprecated in favor of `proxy.ts`
Next.js 16 introduces **`proxy.ts`** as the preferred request interception file convention.

- The blog says `proxy.ts` “replaces `middleware.ts`” for clarity.
- It also notes `middleware.ts` is still available for Edge runtime use cases but is **deprecated** and will be removed later.

Sources:  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#proxyts-formerly-middlewarets  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### What to do
Rename:

- file: `middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts`
- exported function: `middleware` → `proxy`

Codemod:

```bash
npx @next/codemod@latest middleware-to-proxy .
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Team note
If you deliberately depend on Edge-runtime middleware behavior, validate the current proxy/middleware docs carefully before bulk renaming.

Official proxy file convention:  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

---

## 4.2 Async Request APIs from Next.js 15 are still critical
If your app adopted Next.js 15 incompletely, clean this up before or during the 16 migration. In Next.js 15, the following became async:

- `cookies()`
- `headers()`
- `draftMode()`
- `params`
- `searchParams`

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

### Example
Before:

```ts
const token = cookies().get('token')
```

After:

```ts
const token = (await cookies()).get('token')
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

### Codemod
```bash
npx @next/codemod@latest next-async-request-api .
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Why this still matters in a 15 → 16 migration
Next.js 16 continues enforcing async access patterns and extends them in metadata image routes too.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals

---

## 4.3 `revalidateTag()` signature changed
In Next.js 16, `revalidateTag()` now expects a **second argument** representing a `cacheLife` profile for stale-while-revalidate behavior.

Before:

```ts
revalidateTag('blog-posts')
```

After:

```ts
revalidateTag('blog-posts', 'max')
```

Or:

```ts
revalidateTag('products', { expire: 3600 })
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#revalidatetag-updated

### New related APIs
Next.js 16 also adds:

- `updateTag()` — Server Actions only, read-your-writes semantics
- `refresh()` — Server Actions only, refresh uncached data

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#updatetag-new

### Migration advice
Audit all calls to:

- `revalidateTag(...)`
- any custom cache abstraction wrapping it

Decide which semantics you need:

- **SWR-ish invalidation** → `revalidateTag(tag, 'max')`
- **immediate read-your-writes in actions** → `updateTag(tag)`

---

## 4.4 Parallel routes now require explicit `default.js`
Next.js 16 changes parallel route behavior: all parallel route slots now require explicit **`default.js`** files, and builds fail without them.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes

### What to do
For each parallel route slot, add `default.js` (or `.tsx`) that either:

- returns `null`, or
- calls `notFound()`

This is a build-breaker if you use parallel routes and relied on previous implicit behavior.

---

## 4.5 `next build` no longer runs linting; `next lint` removed
Next.js 16 removes the `next lint` command and says `next build` no longer runs linting.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals

### What to do
Move linting fully into explicit package scripts / CI:

```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "eslint ."
  }
}
```

Official codemod:

```bash
npx @next/codemod@canary next-lint-to-eslint-cli .
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Also note
`@next/eslint-plugin-next` now defaults to **ESLint Flat Config**.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes

If your repo still uses legacy `.eslintrc*`, test the migration separately.

---

## 4.6 Removed/renamed config flags
These Next.js 16 changes matter if your repo has old flags in `next.config.*`.

### Removed / renamed
- `experimental.dynamicIO` → **`cacheComponents`**
- `experimental.ppr` removed
- `export const experimental_ppr` removed
- `experimental.turbopack` config location moved to top-level **`turbopack`**
- `serverRuntimeConfig` and `publicRuntimeConfig` removed; use env vars
- AMP support removed
- some `devIndicators` options removed

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals

### Practical checklist
Search your repo for:

- `dynamicIO`
- `experimental.ppr`
- `experimental_ppr`
- `experimental.turbopack`
- `serverRuntimeConfig`
- `publicRuntimeConfig`
- `amp: true`
- `useAmp`
- `devIndicators`

---

## 4.7 `next/image` defaults and restrictions changed
Next.js 16 changes several `next/image` defaults and security behaviors.

### Changed defaults
- `images.minimumCacheTTL`: **60s → 14400s (4 hours)**
- `images.imageSizes`: default removes `16`
- `images.qualities`: default becomes `[75]`
- `images.maximumRedirects`: default becomes **3**
- local IP optimization blocked by default unless `images.dangerouslyAllowLocalIP = true`

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes

### New restriction
Local `src` values with query strings now require `images.localPatterns` to prevent enumeration attacks.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#removals

### Migration advice
Audit:

- custom image loaders
- quality assumptions
- image cache TTL expectations
- internal/private-network image fetches
- local image URLs with query strings

---

## 4.8 Custom webpack config becomes higher risk because Turbopack is now default
This is one of the most important production migration issues.

Next.js 16 makes **Turbopack the default bundler**. Official upgrade docs state:

> If your project has a custom `webpack` configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will **fail** to prevent misconfiguration issues.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### What this means
If your app has a `webpack(config) { ... }` block in `next.config.js/ts`, you cannot assume `next build` will continue working unchanged after the upgrade.

### Your options
1. **Short-term safety:** force webpack in CI/prod while migrating
   ```bash
   next build --webpack
   next dev --webpack
   ```
   - Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable

2. **Long-term target:** port supported needs to top-level `turbopack` config and remove webpack-specific customizations where possible.
   - Turbopack config docs:  
     https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack

### Strong recommendation
Before upgrading production:
- inventory every webpack customization
- classify each as:
  - no longer needed
  - replaceable with standard Next.js behavior
  - portable to Turbopack config
  - blocker requiring temporary `--webpack`

---

## 4.9 `next/font` and legacy APIs
If any older code remains in your repo, Next.js 15 already removed `@next/font` in favor of built-in `next/font`.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

Next.js 16 also deprecates `next/legacy/image`.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#deprecations

---

## 4.10 `NextRequest.geo` and `NextRequest.ip`
Next.js 15 removed `geo` and `ip` on `NextRequest`.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

If you are on Vercel, the documented replacement is:

- `geolocation(request)` from `@vercel/functions`
- `ipAddress(request)` from `@vercel/functions`

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

This is often missed in auth, fraud, or localization middleware/proxy code.

---

## 5) New build tooling defaults and what they mean in practice

## 5.1 Turbopack is now the default bundler
Starting in Next.js 16, Turbopack is stable for both dev and production builds and is the default bundler.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable

### Impact
- faster builds and Fast Refresh
- different compatibility surface than webpack
- custom webpack logic may block adoption

### Opt-out
Use:
```bash
next build --webpack
next dev --webpack
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable

---

## 5.2 Turbopack filesystem caching
Timeline from official sources:

- **15.5.0**: persistent caching experimental on canary releases  
- **16.0.0**: filesystem cache beta with separate build/dev flags  
- **16.1.0**: filesystem caching enabled by default for development

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache

### Config names
```ts
experimental: {
  turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev: true,
  turbopackFileSystemCacheForBuild: true,
}
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache

### Stability level
Official docs say filesystem cache is:
- **stable for development**
- **experimental for production builds**

- Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache

### Team recommendation
- Accept the dev default.
- Treat build cache persistence as an explicit CI optimization, not a blind default.
- Benchmark in your CI/CD and cache `.next` only after validating correctness.

---

## 5.3 Build output and locking behavior changed
Next.js 16 changed tooling behavior around concurrent execution:

- `next dev` and `next build` now use separate output directories
- a lockfile mechanism prevents multiple `next dev` or `next build` instances on the same project

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes

### Why you care
This can affect:
- local dev scripts
- monorepo task runners
- custom build orchestration
- CI jobs sharing workspaces

---

## 5.4 Babel behavior under Turbopack changed
Next.js 16 says Turbopack now **automatically enables Babel if a Babel config is found**; previously it errored hard.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes

### Migration implication
If your app still ships a Babel config:
- you may silently pull Babel back into the pipeline under Turbopack
- builds may behave differently or become slower
- this matters especially if you plan to enable `reactCompiler`

Audit for:
- `.babelrc`
- `babel.config.js`
- package-level Babel configs in monorepos

---

## 5.5 React Compiler support is stable, but not on by default
`reactCompiler` became a stable config in Next.js 16, promoted from `experimental`, but it is **not enabled by default**.

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#react-compiler-support-stable

### Config
```ts
const nextConfig = {
  reactCompiler: true,
}
```

- Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#react-compiler-support-stable

### Recommendation
Do **not** combine:
- Next 15 → 16 migration
- Turbopack migration
- React Compiler enablement

…into one rollout. Keep React Compiler as a separate experiment.

---

## 5.6 Adapters API status
Build Adapters were:
- **alpha** in 16.0  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#build-adapters-api-alpha
- discussed as **stable Adapter API** in 16.2-era cross-platform post  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/nextjs-across-platforms

If you self-host or run on a non-Vercel platform, validate adapter assumptions separately. Do not assume the deployment surface is unchanged from 15.x.

---

## 6) Recommended migration checklist for engineering teams

## Pre-migration audit
- [ ] Record current Next.js, React, Node, TypeScript versions
- [ ] Check if app uses **App Router**, **Pages Router**, or hybrid
- [ ] Search for custom `webpack` config
- [ ] Search for `middleware.ts`
- [ ] Search for async-request API violations:
  - `cookies()`
  - `headers()`
  - `draftMode()`
  - direct sync access to `params` / `searchParams`
- [ ] Search for `revalidateTag(` calls
- [ ] Search for parallel routes without `default.js`
- [ ] Search for removed config flags:
  - `experimental.dynamicIO`
  - `experimental.ppr`
  - `experimental.turbopack`
  - `serverRuntimeConfig`
  - `publicRuntimeConfig`
- [ ] Search for deprecated image config:
  - `images.domains`
  - local query-string image usage
- [ ] Search for `next lint` in package scripts / CI
- [ ] Search for `useAmp`, `amp: true`, `next/legacy/image`, `@next/font`

## Upgrade execution
- [ ] Upgrade infra/runtime to Node 20.9+, TS 5.1+
- [ ] Run official codemod upgrade
- [ ] Apply targeted codemods (`middleware-to-proxy`, `next-lint-to-eslint-cli`, etc.)
- [ ] Keep `--webpack` temporarily if custom webpack exists
- [ ] Fix compile/runtime breaks
- [ ] Update lint scripts and CI steps explicitly

## Validation
- [ ] Production build
- [ ] SSR/ISR/cache revalidation flows
- [ ] auth and session logic
- [ ] proxy/middleware behavior
- [ ] route handlers
- [ ] image optimization
- [ ] CSP/SRI if used
- [ ] observability and instrumentation
- [ ] cold-start and build-time benchmarks

---

## 7) Suggested rollout plan

## Option A: safest path for large production apps
### Release 1
- Upgrade to latest 15.x
- remove deprecations
- make async request APIs fully compliant
- move linting off `next lint`

### Release 2
- Upgrade to 16.2.x
- keep builds on webpack with:
  ```bash
  next build --webpack
  ```
  if you have custom webpack config

### Release 3
- Migrate from webpack to Turbopack after parity validation
- optionally enable build filesystem caching in CI
- later evaluate React Compiler

This is the least risky path.

## Option B: faster path for standard apps
If the app has:
- no custom webpack config
- no exotic Babel setup
- minimal proxy/middleware logic
- no parallel routes
- straightforward image usage

…you can usually go straight to 16.2.x with codemods and targeted regression testing.

---

## 8) High-risk areas to test after the upgrade

### Caching and revalidation
Because Next 15 and 16 both changed caching semantics and APIs, test:
- `fetch` cache behavior
- route handler caching
- `revalidateTag`
- user-facing freshness after writes

Sources:  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#improved-caching-apis

### Routing and request interception
Test:
- rewrites
- redirects
- auth gates
- geo/IP logic replacements
- middleware/proxy runtime assumptions

Sources:  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#proxyts-formerly-middlewarets  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

### Build system
Test:
- custom loaders/plugins
- Babel interactions
- monorepo CSS/PostCSS behavior
- production source maps
- CI caching

Sources:  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2-turbopack  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache

### Images
Test:
- redirects
- internal hosts
- quality assumptions
- cache TTL
- query-string local images

Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#behavior-changes

---

## 9) Concrete command set for the team

### Upgrade packages
```bash
npx @next/codemod upgrade 16
```
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Or general latest upgrade flow
```bash
npx @next/codemod@canary upgrade latest
```
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/upgrading

### Migrate deprecated middleware convention
```bash
npx @next/codemod@latest middleware-to-proxy .
```
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Migrate linting
```bash
npx @next/codemod@canary next-lint-to-eslint-cli .
```
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Temporary webpack fallback if needed
```bash
next build --webpack
next dev --webpack
```
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16#turbopack-stable

---

## 10) Final recommendation

For a production engineering team in mid-2026, the right target is **Next.js 16.2.x stable**, not 16.3 Preview. The biggest migration concerns from 15 → current are:

1. **Node 20.9+ / TS 5.1+ runtime floor**
2. **Turbopack is now the default bundler**
3. **custom webpack configs may make `next build` fail unless you stay on `--webpack` temporarily**
4. **`middleware.ts` is deprecated in favor of `proxy.ts`**
5. **`revalidateTag()` signature changed**
6. **parallel route slots now require explicit `default.js`**
7. **`next lint` is removed; linting must move to ESLint CLI**
8. **`next/image` defaults and security restrictions changed**

Short answer: upgrade production apps from Next.js 15 to **Next.js 16.2.x**, not 16.3 Preview; prepare for **Node 20.9+**, **TypeScript 5.1+**, **Turbopack as the default bundler**, and breaking changes around **`proxy.ts`**, **`revalidateTag(tag, profile)`**, **parallel-route `default.js`**, and removal of **`next lint`**.

gptr.report:
# Migration guide: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to current Next.js (mid-2026)

## Executive summary

As of mid-2026, the current **stable** Next.js release line is **Next.js 16.2.x**. The official blog shows 16.2 as a stable release, while 16.3 was published as preview-oriented posts rather than the stable baseline to target for a production migration in mid-2026.  
Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 , https://nextjs.org/blog , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations

For a production app on Next.js 15, the upgrade to Next.js 16 is not just a package bump. The main issues to plan for are:

- **Node.js minimum is now 20.9+**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- **React and react-dom minimum is now 19**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- **`next build` uses Turbopack by default**.  
  Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16
- **If you still have custom `webpack` config, builds can fail** under the new default.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- **`middleware.ts`/`middleware.js` is renamed to `proxy.ts`/`proxy.js`**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- **`next lint` was removed in Next.js 16**; use the ESLint CLI directly.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- **Async Request APIs become more fully enforced**, especially around `params`, `searchParams`, and related App Router surfaces.  
  Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 , https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

The safest recommendation for most teams is: **upgrade from Next.js 15.x to the latest 16.2.x patch, not to 16.3 preview-era features**.

---

## 1) Recommended target version

### Target
Upgrade to the latest **Next.js 16.2.x stable patch** available in your lockfile policy window.

### Why not target 16.3 first?
The official Next.js blog in mid-2026 presents 16.3 content as preview / upcoming-feature posts, while 16.2 is the clearly stable release line. For production migrations, use the latest stable line unless you have a reason to adopt preview functionality.  
Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-ai-improvements , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations

---

## 2) Runtime and dependency requirements

## Node.js
Next.js 16 requires **Node.js 20.9+** minimum.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Action
Before touching app code, verify:

```bash
node -v
```

If production, CI, Docker images, and developer machines are not on Node 20.9+, upgrade them first.

### Operational implications
Check all of these:
- CI runners
- production containers/base images
- local `.nvmrc` / `.node-version`
- serverless or platform runtime settings
- any build cache keyed by Node version

---

## React
Next.js 16 requires **`react` 19** and **`react-dom` 19** minimum.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

If you are on Next.js 15, you may already have React 19, because the Next.js 15 upgrade guide also states the minimum versions of `react` and `react-dom` are 19.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

### Action
Verify your package versions:

```bash
npm ls next react react-dom
```

---

## 3) Official upgrade path

Next.js provides an official codemod-based upgrade workflow.

### Use the Next.js codemod
Official docs recommend:

```bash
npx @next/codemod upgrade major
```

Or target version 16 specifically:

```bash
npx @next/codemod upgrade 16
```

Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

The codemod may also prompt for additional transforms, including React 19-related changes where applicable.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Recommended process for a production app
1. Upgrade Node runtime to 20.9+.
2. Create a dedicated migration branch.
3. Run the official Next.js codemod.
4. Fix compile-time API changes.
5. Validate local dev and production builds.
6. Run integration and smoke tests against a staging deployment.
7. Cut over only after confirming build behavior under Turbopack.

---

## 4) Biggest breaking changes from 15 to 16

## A. `next build` now uses Turbopack by default

This is the biggest operational change.

Next.js 16 changed `next build` so that **Turbopack is now the default bundler for builds**.  
Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

In Next.js 15.3, Turbopack for production builds was still **alpha** behind `next build --turbopack`.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-3

### What this means
Your build system has changed even if your application code did not.

### High-risk cases
You are at elevated migration risk if your app has:
- `next.config.*` with a custom `webpack(...)` function
- nonstandard loaders/plugins
- direct assumptions about webpack internals
- custom asset handling that depended on webpack behavior

### Official failure mode
The Next.js 16 upgrade guide states that if your project has a custom `webpack` configuration and you run `next build`, **the build will fail** to prevent misconfiguration.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Team guidance
Audit `next.config.js|mjs|ts` for:
- `webpack(config, context) { ... }`
- old `experimental.turbo`
- bundler-specific plugins or aliases

If custom webpack exists, do not assume the app will build after the version bump.

---

## B. `middleware` is renamed to `proxy`

Next.js 16 renames the file convention from **`middleware`** to **`proxy`**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Old
```ts
// middleware.ts
export function middleware(request) { ... }
```

### New
```ts
// proxy.ts
export function proxy(request) { ... }
```

There is also an official codemod for this rename: `middleware-to-proxy`.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

### Team guidance
Search for:
- `middleware.ts`
- `middleware.js`
- references in docs or internal runbooks
- tests that assert middleware naming

Also review matchers and behavior after rename using the new Proxy docs.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

---

## C. Async Request APIs are now more strict / more widely enforced

Next.js 15 introduced **Async Request APIs** as a breaking change, with temporary synchronous compatibility in some paths.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

Next.js 16 continues that direction. The upgrade guide explicitly calls out alignment with Async Request APIs and notes that affected surfaces now receive request values such as `params` and `id` as promises in some APIs.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Common impact areas
- `page.js`
- `layout.js`
- `generateMetadata`
- `searchParams`
- route-adjacent generation APIs
- image generation functions

### Example pattern from official docs
In affected App Router code, `params` may become:

```ts
type Params = Promise<{ slug: string }>
```

and then be used as:

```ts
const { slug } = await params
```

Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

### Team guidance
Search for synchronous access patterns like:
- `params.slug`
- `searchParams.foo`
- metadata functions that destructure sync params

Refactor them to the promise-based shape where required by the v16 docs.

---

## D. Image generation API changed

The Next.js 16 upgrade guide states that, to align with Async Request APIs, image generating functions now receive **`params` and `id` as promises**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Team guidance
If you use Open Graph image generation or similar handlers, audit those files specifically.

---

## E. `next lint` was removed

In Next.js 16:
- **`next lint` is removed**
- the **`eslint` option in `next.config` is removed**

Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

This removal was signaled earlier in Next.js 15.5.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

### Action
Replace usage like:

```bash
next lint
```

with direct ESLint CLI usage, for example:

```bash
eslint .
```

Adjust:
- CI commands
- pre-commit hooks
- package scripts
- docs/runbooks

---

## F. Some config options were removed or moved

The official v16 upgrade guide lists several config changes.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Important ones to audit

#### 1. `experimental.turbo` → `turbopack`
The older config path is no longer the correct model. Use the top-level Turbopack config.  
Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 , https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack

#### 2. `experimental.turbopackFileSystemCacheForDev` removed
This old experimental setting should be removed.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

#### 3. `bundlePagesExternals` removed
Remove references if present.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

#### 4. `cacheHandler` moved to top level
If you used an experimental location, update it to top-level `cacheHandler`.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

#### 5. `dynamicIO` removed in favor of top-level `cacheComponents`
This is a meaningful caching model change.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

#### 6. `devIndicators.appIsrStatus` and `devIndicators.buildActivity` removed
Delete these settings if present.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

#### 7. AMP support removed
If any app area still depends on AMP, that requires a separate migration plan.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

---

## 5) New build-tooling defaults and what they mean

## Turbopack is now the default bundler
The Next.js 16 release blog describes Turbopack as stable and the default bundler for all apps.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### Practical implications
- build performance characteristics change
- source maps and debug workflows may differ
- module resolution edge cases may surface
- unsupported webpack customizations must be removed or rewritten

## Turbopack file system caching
In 16.1, Turbopack file-system caching for `next dev` became **stable and on by default**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1

This matters for developer experience and can change local cold-start characteristics.

## Build debugging improvements
Next.js 16.2 added `--inspect` support for `next start`, which can help production-like debugging.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2

### Recommended build validation matrix
After the upgrade, test all of:
- `next dev`
- `next build`
- `next start`
- CI build in a clean environment
- staging deploy on your production hosting platform

---

## 6) Caching changes you should treat as breaking

Next.js 16 changed parts of the caching API model.

## `revalidateTag()` changed
The Next.js 16 release notes say **`revalidateTag()` now requires a `cacheLife` profile as the second argument** for stale-while-revalidate behavior.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### Example concept
Old assumptions about calling `revalidateTag(tag)` may no longer be correct for your intended semantics.

## `updateTag()` was introduced
Next.js 16 introduces **`updateTag()`** as part of the refined caching model.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

## `cacheComponents`
Next.js 16 promotes **`cacheComponents`** as the top-level model replacing older experimental knobs like `dynamicIO`.  
Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16 , https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 , https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents

### Team guidance
Audit:
- `revalidateTag(...)`
- `revalidatePath(...)`
- route handlers or server actions doing invalidation
- any experimental caching flags in `next.config.*`

---

## 7) Hosting/runtime considerations

The official deployment docs state that to run full-featured Next.js, your platform needs **a Node.js server**; one `next start` process handles features such as:
- Server Components
- ISR
- PPR
- Cache Components
- Server Actions
- Proxy
- `after()`

Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/deploying-to-platforms

### Why this matters
If your current production deployment relies on older assumptions around mostly-static output or older middleware behavior, revisit platform compatibility before rollout.

Also note that Next.js 16.2 introduced a **stable Adapter API** for broader platform support.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/nextjs-across-platforms

---

## 8) Suggested migration checklist for an engineering team

## Phase 1: pre-upgrade audit
- Confirm app is on the latest Next.js 15 patch first.
- Confirm Node runtime can move to **20.9+**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- Confirm React can move or stay on **19 / 19**.  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- Inventory:
  - custom `webpack` config
  - `middleware.ts/js`
  - `next lint` usage
  - `next.config.*` experimental flags
  - App Router code using sync `params` / `searchParams`
  - cache invalidation APIs

## Phase 2: automated upgrade
- Run:
  ```bash
  npx @next/codemod upgrade 16
  ```
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods
- If not handled automatically, run the middleware rename codemod:
  ```bash
  npx @next/codemod@latest middleware-to-proxy .
  ```
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/codemods

## Phase 3: manual code remediation
- Rename `middleware` to `proxy`
- Refactor sync request API access
- Update image generation handlers
- Replace `next lint` in scripts/CI
- Remove or migrate deprecated config keys
- Resolve any custom webpack incompatibilities

## Phase 4: build and runtime validation
- Local:
  - `next dev`
  - `next build`
  - `next start`
- CI:
  - clean install
  - clean build cache
- Staging:
  - auth flows
  - rewrites/redirects
  - route handlers
  - server actions
  - cache invalidation
  - OG image generation
  - error handling

## Phase 5: production rollout
- deploy behind feature flag / canary if possible
- monitor:
  - build times
  - memory use
  - SSR latency
  - error rate
  - cache invalidation behavior
  - route matching in Proxy

---

## 9) Specific file/API search list for your repo

Run targeted searches before the migration:

```bash
grep -R "middleware" .
grep -R "next lint" .
grep -R "webpack(" .
grep -R "experimental.turbo" .
grep -R "dynamicIO" .
grep -R "revalidateTag(" .
grep -R "searchParams" app
grep -R "params." app
```

Also inspect:
- `next.config.js|mjs|ts`
- `package.json`
- CI pipeline config
- Dockerfiles
- image generation handlers
- auth/proxy logic

---

## 10) Recommended package/script baseline

## Package versions
Move to:
- `next`: latest **16.2.x stable patch**
- `react`: `19`
- `react-dom`: `19`

Sources: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2 , https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

## Scripts
Replace old lint script patterns:

```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "dev": "next dev",
    "build": "next build",
    "start": "next start",
    "lint": "eslint ."
  }
}
```

`next lint` should not remain in Next 16.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

---

## 11) What changed since Next.js 15 that matters most operationally

### In Next.js 15
- React 19 minimum
- Async Request APIs introduced as a breaking change
- Turbopack build was still alpha (`next build --turbopack`)

Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15 , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-3

### In Next.js 16
- Node minimum bumped to 20.9+
- `next build` switched to Turbopack by default
- Middleware renamed to Proxy
- `next lint` removed
- caching APIs refined
- several experimental config flags removed or stabilized

Sources: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16 , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### In 16.1 / 16.2
- `next dev` file-system caching stable and on by default in 16.1  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1
- 16.2 adds major Turbopack fixes and `next start --inspect`  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2

---

## Final recommendation

For a production migration from Next.js 15 in mid-2026:

1. **Target the latest Next.js 16.2.x stable patch**.  
2. **Upgrade Node to 20.9+ first**.  
3. **Assume your build system changed**, because `next build` now defaults to Turbopack.  
4. **Audit custom webpack config immediately**.  
5. **Rename `middleware` to `proxy`**.  
6. **Remove `next lint` usage**.  
7. **Fix Async Request API usage and caching API changes before rollout**.

Short answer: **Upgrade to the latest stable Next.js 16.2.x, require Node 20.9+ and React 19, expect Turbopack to be the new default for `next build`, and plan for breaking changes around `proxy`, async request APIs, linting, and caching.**

**Briefing: State of the 2026 FIFA World Cup as of 4 July 2026**

## Executive summary

The 2026 FIFA World Cup is now into the **Round of 16**, the first World Cup to use a **48-team format** with **12 groups of four**, a **Round of 32**, and **104 matches** overall. FIFA says the tournament runs from **11 June to 19 July 2026** across **16 host cities** in **Canada, Mexico and the United States**. The top two teams in each group plus the **eight best third-placed teams** advanced to the knockout phase.  
Sources: FIFA format explainer: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/article-fifa-world-cup-2026-mexico-canada-usa-new-format-tournament-football-soccer ; FIFA tournament overview: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/fifa-world-cup-2026-hosts-cities-dates-usa-mexico-canada ; FIFA knockout schedule: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

As of today, all **16 teams in the Round of 16 are set**. The most notable knockout upsets so far are **Paraguay eliminating Germany on penalties** and **Morocco knocking out the Netherlands on penalties**. Other standout storylines include **Canada reaching the last 16 on home soil**, **Mexico remaining perfect**, **Belgium surviving after extra time**, and **Argentina needing extra time to edge Cabo Verde**.  
Sources: FIFA knockout schedule/results hub: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket ; FIFA matchday preview and recap pages cited below.

---

## 1) Tournament format and structure

FIFA changed the tournament from the old 32-team model to a **48-team competition** for 2026. The approved structure is **12 groups of four**, with the **top two in each group** plus the **eight best third-placed teams** advancing to a new **Round of 32**. The eventual champion must now play **eight matches** rather than seven. FIFA also states the tournament has **104 matches** and is being staged across **three host nations**.  
Sources:  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/article-fifa-world-cup-2026-mexico-canada-usa-new-format-tournament-football-soccer  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/fifa-world-cup-2026-hosts-cities-dates-usa-mexico-canada

FIFA’s knockout-stage schedule confirms the progression from **Round of 32** to **Round of 16**, then **quarter-finals** on **9-11 July**, **semi-finals** on **14-15 July**, the **bronze final** on **18 July**, and the **final** on **19 July** at **New York New Jersey Stadium**.  
Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

---

## 2) How the knockout stage has unfolded so far

### Round of 32 results completed before today’s Round of 16

FIFA’s official knockout hub lists the following Round of 32 results and dates:

### Monday, 29 June

- **Netherlands 1-1 Morocco (PSO 2-3)** — Estadio Monterrey

Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

### Tuesday, 30 June

- **Côte d’Ivoire 1-2 Norway** — Dallas Stadium

Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

### Wednesday, 1 July
- **England 2-1 Congo DR** — Atlanta Stadium  
- **USA 2-0 Bosnia and Herzegovina** — San Francisco Bay Area Stadium  
- **Belgium 3-2 Senegal (AET)** — Seattle Stadium  
Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

### Thursday, 2 July

### Friday, 3 July

These were listed by FIFA as the final Round-of-32 fixtures, and FIFA’s later reporting confirms the winners were **Egypt, Argentina and Colombia**.

- Preview: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/matchday-23-preview-round-of-32

- Argentina report: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/argentina-cabo-verde-match-report-highlights

---

## 3) Notable results and upsets

### Paraguay eliminate Germany
This is the clearest upset of the Round of 32 so far. FIFA’s knockout schedule records **Germany 1-1 Paraguay, Paraguay winning 4-3 on penalties** on **29 June** in Boston. FIFA’s matchday preview on 2 July explicitly referred to this as a **“shock elimination.”**

- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/matchday-23-preview-round-of-32

### Morocco knock out the Netherlands
Morocco beat the Netherlands after a **1-1 draw**, winning the shootout **3-2** on **29 June** in Monterrey. Given the Netherlands’ pedigree and Morocco’s bracket path, this qualifies as another major bracket-disrupting result.  
Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

### Argentina pushed to extra time by Cabo Verde
Argentina needed **extra time** to beat Cabo Verde **3-2** on **3 July** in Miami. FIFA’s report says Argentina led twice, Cabo Verde equalised twice, and the match was finally decided by a **Diney Borges own goal in the 111th minute** from a Lionel Messi corner. Goals were: **Messi 29', Lautaro Martinez 92', Borges (OG) 111'** for Argentina; **Deroy Duarte 59'** and **Sidny Lopes Cabral 103'** for Cabo Verde.  
Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/argentina-cabo-verde-match-report-highlights

### Egypt make history
FIFA’s related coverage on 4 July reports that **Egypt beat Australia in a shootout** to reach the last 16, describing it as a **historic step**. FIFA’s Argentina report also confirms the next-round pairing as **Argentina vs Egypt**, which verifies Egypt advanced from that tie.  
Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/argentina-cabo-verde-match-report-highlights

### Belgium survive after extra time
Belgium advanced by beating Senegal **3-2 after extra time** on **1 July** in Seattle. FIFA’s knockout hub and roundup coverage both describe Belgium as having to dig deep and rally.  
Sources:  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/bosnia-england-belgium-usa-review-highlights

### Mexico stay perfect
FIFA’s 30 June roundup says **Mexico remained perfect** by beating Ecuador **2-0**, and the knockout hub confirms the result and date.  
Sources:  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/matchday-20-round-up-review-highlights

### France cruise, Spain impress
France beat Sweden **3-0** on 30 June, with FIFA highlighting a **Mbappe double**. Spain beat Austria **3-0** on 2 July, and FIFA’s coverage described Spain as advancing comfortably.  
Sources:  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/france-sweden-review-highlights  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/spain-austria-match-report-highlights  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

---

## 4) Teams that have advanced to the current round

As of today, the **Round of 16 field is complete**. From FIFA’s official bracket, the teams through are:

## 5) Upcoming fixtures

FIFA’s official knockout-stage schedule lists the Round of 16 fixtures as follows.

## Round of 16

# Recap: Super Bowl LX and the 2026 NBA Finals

The two marquee U.S. championships of the 2025-26 sports season both produced title runs with real historical weight: the Seattle Seahawks won Super Bowl LX over the New England Patriots, and the New York Knicks won the 2026 NBA Finals over the San Antonio Spurs. In both cases, the champions did more than just win — they ended long waits, set notable records, and produced outcomes that stand out in league history.

## Super Bowl LX

### Matchup, date, and score
Super Bowl LX was played on **February 8, 2026**, and matched the **Seattle Seahawks** against the **New England Patriots**. Seattle won **29-13**. NFL coverage identifies it as Seattle’s **second Super Bowl title** in franchise history.  
Sources:  
- NFL Super Bowl hub: https://www.nfl.com/super-bowl  
- NFL recap: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win  
- NFL analysis: https://www.nfl.com/news/sam-darnold-silences-skeptics-as-seahawks-dominate-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx

### MVP
The game’s MVP was **Jason Myers**, Seattle’s kicker. NFL reporting says he became the **first kicker ever to win Super Bowl MVP**. He also set a **Super Bowl record with five field goals** and scored **17 of Seattle’s 29 points**.  
Source:  
- https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-jason-myers-first-200-point-scorer-record-five-field-goals-super-bowl-lx

### What made it historically significant
Several elements made the result notable:

1. **Seattle won its second Lombardi Trophy**  
   NFL.com explicitly framed the win as the **second Super Bowl title in franchise history**, putting the 2025 Seahawks alongside the 2013 title team in club lore.  
   Source: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win

2. **The MVP result was unprecedented**  
   Myers became the **first kicker in Super Bowl history to win MVP**, an extraordinary break from the usual quarterback- and skill-position-heavy pattern of the award.  
   Source: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-jason-myers-first-200-point-scorer-record-five-field-goals-super-bowl-lx

3. **A record-setting kicking performance**  
   Myers’ **five field goals** were a **Super Bowl record**, and NFL.com also noted that his season total made him the **first player in NFL history to score 200-plus points in a single season including playoffs**.  
   Source: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-jason-myers-first-200-point-scorer-record-five-field-goals-super-bowl-lx

4. **Seattle’s defense defined the game**  
   NFL coverage described the Seahawks’ 29-13 win as one of the most impressive defensive Super Bowl performances in memory, with the defense overwhelming New England and holding a high-powered Patriots offense in check.  
   Sources:  
   - https://www.nfl.com/news/sam-darnold-silences-skeptics-as-seahawks-dominate-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx  
   - https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-cb-devon-witherspoon-shines-brightest-for-seattle-s-defense-in-super-bowl-lx-win-over-patriots

### Sports-desk takeaway
The cleanest framing is that **Seattle beat New England 29-13 on Feb. 8, 2026, behind a dominant defense and a history-making special-teams MVP**. The Seahawks did not merely win another title game; they produced the first Super Bowl MVP by a kicker and got a record five-field-goal night from Jason Myers.

---

## 2026 NBA Finals

### Matchup, dates, and series result
The **2026 NBA Finals** matched the **New York Knicks** against the **San Antonio Spurs**. The series ran from **June 3 to June 13, 2026**, and New York won the championship **4-1**. The clincher came in **Game 5 on June 13**, when the Knicks beat the Spurs **94-90**.  
Sources:  
- NBA Finals hub: https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/nba-finals  
- NBA recap/live updates: https://www.nba.com/news/live-updates-2026-nba-finals-game-5-knicks-seek-first-title-since-1973  
- NBA championship overview: https://www.nba.com/news/how-the-knicks-won-the-2026-nba-championship

### MVP
The **2026 NBA Finals MVP** was **Jalen Brunson**, who won the **Bill Russell Trophy**. In the closeout game, he scored a **Knicks Finals-record 45 points**, including **15 in the fourth quarter**.  
Source:  
- https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp

### What made it historically significant
This series carried multiple historic angles:

1. **The Knicks won their first NBA title since 1973**  
   NBA.com repeatedly identified the championship as New York’s **first in 53 years**, ending one of the most famous droughts in American pro sports. It was also the franchise’s **third NBA championship** overall.  
   Sources:  
   - https://www.nba.com/news/starting-5-knicks-complete-historic-run-win-first-nba-title-since-1973  
   - https://www.nba.com/news/how-the-knicks-won-the-2026-nba-championship

2. **Brunson delivered a record closeout performance**  
   Brunson’s **45 points in Game 5** were a **Knicks Finals record**, and NBA.com described the effort as one of the more convincing closeout performances in Finals history.  
   Source: https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp

3. **The Finals were historically clutch**  
   NBA.com called the 2026 Finals “historically clutch,” noting that New York repeatedly erased large deficits. Brunson led the series with **22 clutch points**, the most by any player in the Finals in the last 15 years according to NBA.com’s play-by-play era framing.  
   Source: https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-finals-prove-historically-clutch

4. **Game 4 produced an NBA Finals record comeback**  
   Down **29 points**, the Knicks rallied to win **Game 4, 107-106**, which NBA.com labeled an **NBA Finals record** comeback. That win effectively turned the series into an all-but-finished 3-1 lead.  
   Source: https://www.nba.com/news/knicks-complete-nba-finals-record-29-point-comeback-to-seal-game-4-win-over-spurs

5. **New York completed an unusual “double”**  
   NBA.com said the Knicks became the **first team to win an NBA “double”**, referring to capturing both the **NBA Cup** and the **NBA championship** in the same season.  
   Source: https://www.nba.com/news/4-takeaways-knicks-spurs-game-5

6. **The playoff run itself was historically strong**  
   NBA.com said the Knicks finished the postseason with a **14.9 point differential per game**, the **best in NBA playoff history**, while also stringing together **13 straight playoff wins** before Game 3 of the Finals.  
   Source: https://www.nba.com/news/5-stats-that-define-new-york-knicks-championship-run

### Sports-desk takeaway
The sharpest summary is that **the Knicks beat the Spurs 4-1, clinched with a 94-90 Game 5 win on June 13, 2026, and ended a 53-year title drought behind Finals MVP Jalen Brunson**. The series will be remembered for Brunson’s scoring, New York’s comeback habit, and that record **29-point rally in Game 4**.

---

## Bottom line

For a sports desk, the common thread is that both championships delivered more than a winner:

- **Super Bowl LX:** Seahawks over Patriots, **29-13**, on **Feb. 8, 2026**; **Jason Myers** was MVP after becoming the **first kicker ever to win the award** and setting a **Super Bowl record with five field goals**.  
- **2026 NBA Finals:** Knicks over Spurs, **4-1**, clinching **94-90** on **June 13, 2026**; **Jalen Brunson** won Finals MVP as New York captured its **first NBA title since 1973** and completed a postseason remembered for historic comeback wins.

Short answer: **Super Bowl LX — Seahawks 29, Patriots 13; MVP Jason Myers. 2026 NBA Finals — Knicks beat Spurs 4-1, closing with a 94-90 Game 5; Finals MVP Jalen Brunson.**

- Best Director

# NASA Artemis II mission summary

## Overview

Artemis II was NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission and the first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years. NASA launched the mission on **April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT** from **Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center, Florida**, using the **Space Launch System (SLS)** rocket and the **Orion** spacecraft. The mission ended with **splashdown in the Pacific Ocean off San Diego on April 10, 2026, at about 8:07 p.m. EDT (5:07 p.m. PDT)**. NASA described the flight as an approximately **10-day** mission around the Moon and back.  
Sources: NASA launch-day updates: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; NASA FAQ: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA Flight Day 10 update: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown

## Crew and the historic firsts

The Artemis II crew consisted of:

- **Reid Wiseman** (NASA), **commander**
- **Victor Glover** (NASA), **pilot**
- **Christina Koch** (NASA), **mission specialist**
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency), **mission specialist**  
Source: NASA crew page: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew

The mission’s crew carried several historic firsts tied to a lunar mission:

- **Victor Glover** became the **first Black astronaut on a lunar mission**.  
- **Christina Koch** became the **first woman on a lunar mission**.  
- **Jeremy Hansen** became the **first Canadian on a lunar mission**.  
NASA identified Artemis II as the first crewed flight of the Artemis program and positioned the crew as the team that would “venture around the Moon,” with these milestone selections announced as part of the Artemis II crew assignment.  
Sources: NASA crew page: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew ; NASA press release naming the crew: https://www.nasa.gov/press-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis

## Launch, flight profile, and return

NASA’s launch blog states the SLS/Orion stack lifted off on **April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT**. After launch, the mission included early-Earth-orbit checkouts, a **proximity operations demonstration** using the **Interim Cryogenic Propulsion Stage (ICPS)** as a target, translunar injection, a lunar flyby, and return to Earth.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates

NASA’s mission materials said Artemis II would travel roughly **685,000 miles**; later in-flight NASA updates refined the expected total to **695,081 miles from launch to splashdown**, while the Flight Day 10 update reported the completed journey at **694,481 miles**. The discrepancy reflects NASA’s use of updated in-flight navigation and final mission accounting.  
Sources: NASA crew page/poster text: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew ; NASA FAQ: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA Flight Day 10 update: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown

NASA said Orion would pass within **4,070 miles of the lunar surface** at closest approach and reach a maximum distance of **252,760 miles from Earth**.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions

## What the mission accomplished

Artemis II’s central purpose was to prove that NASA’s deep-space human transportation system works with astronauts aboard. NASA stated the mission’s **primary goal** was a **crewed test flight in lunar space**. The agency’s mission-priorities document lists five broad priorities:

1. **Crew** — demonstrate that systems and teams can sustain the astronauts through flight and return.  
2. **Systems** — demonstrate operations essential to a crewed lunar campaign, from ground systems to in-space systems.  
3. **Hardware and Data** — recover hardware and data for assessment before later missions.  
4. **Emergency Operations** — demonstrate emergency capabilities and associated operations, including rescue procedures where practical.  
5. **Data and Subsystems** — complete additional objectives to verify subsystems and validate data.  
Source: NASA Artemis II priorities/objectives PDF: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/artemis-ii-mission-prioirities-objectives-.pdf

In practice, NASA says Artemis II accomplished the following:

- **Tested Orion’s life-support system** with crew aboard in deep space.  
- **Demonstrated manual piloting and crew operations**, including proximity operations with the ICPS.  
- **Validated propulsion, navigation, communications, thermal, and power systems** in the actual deep-space environment.  
- **Performed a lunar flyby** and returned safely using a free-return trajectory.  
- **Completed re-entry, splashdown, and recovery operations**, giving NASA post-flight hardware and operational data for later Artemis missions.  
Sources: NASA FAQ: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA crew page: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew ; NASA Flight Day 10 update: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown ; NASA Artemis II priorities/objectives PDF: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/artemis-ii-mission-prioirities-objectives-.pdf

NASA also tied the mission to specific science and operations tasks, including **lunar observations**, **human health studies**, and the validation of procedures that will support future lunar surface missions.  
Sources: NASA FAQ: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA reference guide: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/a2-reference-guide-012825.pdf

## Records and notable milestones

### 1) Farthest distance from Earth reached by humans
NASA stated before and during the mission that Artemis II was expected to surpass the Apollo 13 record, and later said the crew reached a **maximum distance of 252,760 miles from Earth**, about **4,105 miles farther than Apollo 13**. NASA’s post-mission communications describe this as a new record for the **farthest distance traveled by humans away from Earth**.  
Sources: NASA launch-day updates: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; NASA FAQ: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA Artemis II streaming-record article, “NASA’s Artemis Program” section: https://www.nasa.gov/general/nasas-artemis-ii-breaks-agency-streaming-record

### 2) First crewed lunar flyby in over 50 years
NASA repeatedly described Artemis II as the **first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years** and the first humans sent toward the Moon since **1972**.  
Sources: NASA Artemis II multimedia page: https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-multimedia ; NASA Flight Day 10 update: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown

### 3) NASA streaming and audience records
NASA reported that Artemis II also set agency media-engagement records. The agency said its live coverage drew **more than 149.4 million views** across NASA-owned platforms over the launch, lunar flyby, and splashdown. NASA further reported:
- **Launch broadcast peak:** **3,662,554** viewers
- **Lunar flyby peak:** **1,471,069** concurrent viewers
- **Splashdown peak:** **3,838,418** viewers  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/general/nasas-artemis-ii-breaks-agency-streaming-record

## Why Artemis II matters for the Artemis program

Artemis II was not a landing mission; its importance was systems validation. NASA says Artemis II built directly on **Artemis I**, the uncrewed 2022 test flight, by proving that Orion, SLS, and the associated ground and recovery systems perform properly **with astronauts aboard in deep space**. That matters because Artemis II retired key risks before future missions put astronauts into more demanding roles in lunar orbit and on the lunar surface.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions

NASA’s own framing is that Artemis II verifies the capabilities needed for **Artemis III and beyond**:
- It validates **life-support and crew-survival systems** for longer missions.
- It exercises **manual operations**, **communications at lunar distances**, and **re-entry/recovery**.
- It returns hardware and operational data to refine later missions.
- It supports the broader Artemis goal of a **sustained human presence on the Moon** and eventually **missions to Mars**.  
Sources: NASA FAQ: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA crew page: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew ; NASA Artemis II priorities/objectives PDF: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/artemis-ii-mission-prioirities-objectives-.pdf ; NASA Artemis overview: https://www.nasa.gov/humans-in-space/artemis/

NASA’s post-mission messaging also explicitly presents Artemis II as proof that the agency can now move to “increasingly challenging missions” under Artemis, including exploration of more of the Moon, work toward a long-term lunar presence, and preparation for eventual crewed Mars expeditions.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/general/nasas-artemis-ii-breaks-agency-streaming-record

## Bottom line

Artemis II launched on **April 1, 2026**, and returned on **April 10, 2026**, carrying **Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen** on the first crewed lunar flyby in over 50 years. It marked the **first woman, first Black astronaut, and first Canadian** assigned to and flown on a lunar mission; tested Orion and SLS with astronauts in deep space; set a new human-distance record of **252,760 miles from Earth**; and cleared a major technical and operational hurdle for **Artemis III and later lunar missions**.  

Short answer: **Artemis II flew from April 1 to April 10, 2026, proved NASA’s crewed Moon system in deep space, set a new human-distance record, and positioned Artemis III and later missions to move from test flight to sustained lunar exploration.**
