# NASA Artemis II: Mission Summary for a Science Desk

## Overview

NASA’s **Artemis II** mission was the first crewed Artemis flight and the first mission to send humans around the Moon since **Apollo 17 in 1972**. The mission launched on **April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT** from **Launch Complex 39B** at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida, and returned with a Pacific splashdown off the coast of California on **April 10, 2026, at 8:07 p.m. EDT**. NASA describes it as an **approximately 10-day** mission around the Moon and back.  
Sources: NASA launch update https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; NASA re-entry update https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-re-entry-live-updates ; NASA Artemis II overview https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; NASA FAQ https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions

## Launch and return timeline

Artemis II lifted off aboard NASA’s **Space Launch System (SLS)** rocket with the **Orion** spacecraft on top. After Earth-orbit maneuvers and a manual piloting demonstration near the upper stage, Orion headed for the Moon. The crew performed a **far-side lunar flyby on April 6, 2026**, then began the return leg to Earth. The mission ended with re-entry and splashdown on **April 10, 2026**, followed by recovery operations in the Pacific.  
Sources: NASA launch update https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; NASA press kit https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit ; NASA lunar flyby update https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates ; NASA re-entry update https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-re-entry-live-updates

## Crew and the historic firsts

The Artemis II crew consisted of:

- **Reid Wiseman** (NASA), commander  
- **Victor Glover** (NASA), pilot  
- **Christina Koch** (NASA), mission specialist  
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency), mission specialist  
Sources: NASA crew page https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew/ ; NASA Artemis II special page https://www3.nasa.gov/specials/artemis-ii ; NASA mission page https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii

The crew represented several milestone firsts for lunar exploration:

- **Christina Koch** became the **first woman** assigned to and flown on a lunar mission under Artemis II.  
- **Victor Glover** became the **first person of color** to fly on a lunar mission.  
- **Jeremy Hansen** became the **first Canadian** to go to the Moon and, according to the Canadian Space Agency, the **first non-American** to go on a lunar mission.  
Sources: NASA crew page https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew/ ; Smithsonian summary of NASA announcement https://airandspace.si.edu/stories/editorial/meet-crew-artemis-ii ; CSA Artemis II page https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/missions/artemis-ii ; CSA daily logbook https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/missions/artemis-ii/daily-logbook.asp

## What the mission was designed to accomplish

Artemis II was not a landing mission. Its purpose was to test the full human-rated deep-space transportation system with astronauts aboard before NASA attempts lunar surface missions. NASA’s stated objectives included:

- validating **Orion’s life-support and crew survival systems** in deep space;  
- demonstrating **systems and operations essential to a crewed lunar campaign**;  
- testing **manual piloting** during proximity operations after upper-stage separation;  
- validating **propulsion, reconfiguration, and flight operations** needed for Earth departure and return;  
- demonstrating **re-entry, splashdown, and recovery** with crew aboard;  
- retrieving hardware and mission data for later analysis.  
Sources: NASA Artemis II priorities/objectives PDF https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/artemis-ii-mission-prioirities-objectives-.pdf?emrc=9fdebb ; NASA press kit https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit ; NASA FAQ https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA SLS reference guide https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/sls-5558-artemis-ii-sls-reference-guide.pdf

## What Artemis II accomplished

By NASA’s own postflight summary, the crew achieved the mission’s primary goals. These included:

- testing Orion’s **life-support systems** with astronauts aboard in deep space;  
- **manually piloting Orion** during the proximity operations demonstration;  
- carrying out maneuvers to send Orion toward the Moon and adjust its course;  
- conducting a **lunar flyby** with views of the Moon’s far side;  
- completing a **safe high-speed re-entry and ocean recovery**.  
Sources: NASA press kit https://www.nasa.gov/artemis-ii-press-kit ; NASA launch coverage page https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-2/nasa-sets-coverage-for-artemis-ii-moon-mission ; NASA postflight assessment https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments ; crew news conference listing with mission summary https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hG44qIy31ec

The mission also carried out planned science and observation activities during the flyby. NASA said the crew observed an **Earthrise** as Orion emerged from behind the Moon, saw an **“Earthset”** as Earth dropped below the lunar horizon, and observed a **solar eclipse from space** as the Moon blocked the Sun for about an hour, allowing observations of the solar corona.  
Sources: NASA flyby updates https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-crew-ready-for-lunar-flyby

## Records and milestones

The clearest mission record was distance from Earth. NASA announced that on **April 6, 2026**, the Artemis II crew surpassed the long-standing record set by **Apollo 13** in 1970 for the **farthest distance from Earth reached by humans**. NASA’s mission blog gave the new maximum distance as **252,756 miles from Earth**.  
Sources: NASA news release https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight ; NASA flight day 6 update https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates

Artemis II was also the **first human lunar flyby mission since Apollo 17**, restoring crewed deep-space operations beyond low Earth orbit after more than five decades.  
Sources: NASA Artemis II page https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; NASA FAQ https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions

A secondary, public-engagement milestone: NASA reported that live coverage of Artemis II drew **more than 149.4 million views** across NASA-owned platforms, which the agency said was an **agency streaming record**.  
Source: NASA https://www.nasa.gov/general/nasas-artemis-ii-breaks-agency-streaming-record

## What Artemis II means for the Artemis program

Artemis II was the program’s decisive crewed systems test. NASA says the mission **paved the way for Artemis III**, the first Artemis landing mission, by validating the SLS-Orion architecture with people aboard in the actual deep-space environment. In June 2026, NASA said **Artemis III is planned for 2027** and explicitly described it as building on the successful Artemis II flight completed in April.  
Sources: NASA Artemis FAQ https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/faq-nasas-artemis-campaign-and-recent-updates ; NASA Artemis III news release https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-marches-toward-artemis-iii-mission-in-2027-names-crew-members ; NASA Artemis III mission page https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-iii

NASA also reported after splashdown that engineers began detailed analysis of **Orion, SLS, and ground systems** to assess performance and feed those results into later missions. That is central to Artemis’ stepwise architecture: Artemis II reduces risk for landing missions by validating crew operations, spacecraft performance, recovery procedures, and mission control workflows before attempting a surface expedition.  
Sources: NASA postflight assessment https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments ; NASA Artemis overview https://www.nasa.gov/humans-in-space/artemis ; NASA Artemis III page https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-iii

Programmatically, Artemis II confirmed that Artemis is no longer just a hardware development effort or an uncrewed demonstration. It is now an operational human exploration campaign with international participation, a tested deep-space crew vehicle, and a direct bridge to lunar surface missions.

## Bottom line

**Artemis II launched on April 1, 2026, and returned on April 10, 2026; flew Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen; made Koch the first woman, Glover the first person of color, and Hansen the first Canadian and first non-American on a lunar mission; validated Orion and SLS with crew in deep space; set a new human-distance-from-Earth record of 252,756 miles; and cleared the way for Artemis III’s planned 2027 lunar landing attempt.**
